author: Dr. Lizard
title: What is the pathophysiology of hyponatremia in the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuresis?
answer: SIAD involves AVP secretion without osmotic or hemodynamic stimuli, causing water retention in euvolemic patients.
detail: Hyponatremia typically reflects water excess relative to body sodium and potassium, impairing electrolyte-free water excretion.
AVP activates vasopressin 2 receptors in collecting ducts, promoting water retention and reducing aquaresis.
In hypovolemic and hypervolemic disorders, AVP release is triggered by reduced effective arterial blood volume.
Hyponatremia requires water intake to exceed losses from kidneys and other routes, regardless of mechanism.
releaseNote: The pathophysiology of hyponatremia in the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuresis involves the secretion of antidiuretic hormone (AVP) without osmotic or hemodynamic stimuli, leading to water retention and impaired electrolyte-free water excretion in euvolemic patients.
